# Visualising Distribution

## Overview

Two new statistical graphic methods for visualizing distribution will be covered in this section. 

1. **Ridgeline plots**: Show distributions for multiple groups stacked along a shared axis, using `ggridges`. 
2. **Raincloud plots**: Combine a half-density, boxplot, and dot plot into one layered graphic, using `ggdist`. 

Both reveal richer distributional shape such as **skewness**, **multimodality**, and **tail behaviour**.

---

## Setting Up


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pacman::p_load(ggdist, ggridges, ggthemes,
               colorspace, tidyverse)
```
:::


| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `ggridges` | Ridgeline plots via `geom_density_ridges()` and variants |
| `ggdist` | Half-eye, dot plots, uncertainty visualisation |
| `ggthemes` | Extra themes, e.g. `theme_economist()` for publication-ready look |
| `colorspace` | Colour manipulation via `lighten()`, `darken()`, palette tools |
| `tidyverse` | Data wrangling (`dplyr`, `tidyr`), and base plotting (`ggplot2`) |

## Importing Data


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
exam <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")
glimpse(exam)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…
```


:::
:::


The dataset has **322 students** across 9 classes (3A–3I) with scores in
English, Maths, and Science. Variables `CLASS`, `GENDER`, and `RACE` serve
as grouping factors throughout this chapter.

---

## Visualising Distribution with Ridgeline Plot
A [**ridgeline plot**](https://www.data-to-viz.com/graph/ridgeline.html) (formerly called a Joyplot) shows the distribution of a numeric variable for several groups, stacked with a slight overlap along the y-axis.

> **When to use ridgeline plots:**
>
> - **5 or more groups** to compare (if we have fewer groups, use violin/boxplot instead)
> - Existence of **natural ordering** to the groups (e.g. class ranking)
> - Reveal **shape/distribution differences** across groups, not just means

In the Exam dataset used here, 9 classes (3A–3I) are a perfect candidate.

### Basic Ridgeline Plot

Distribution of **English scores** across all 9 classes using `geom_density_ridges()`. The classes are ordered 3A to 3I (best to weakest by class assignment).

:::: panel-tabset

#### The plot

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![English score distributions by class - basic ridgeline plot.](Hands-on_Ex04_files/figure-html/fig-ridgeline-basic-1.png){#fig-ridgeline-basic width=672}
:::
:::


#### The code

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS)) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    scale          = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    bandwidth      = 3.4,
    fill           = lighten("#7097BB", .3),
    color          = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name   = "English Score",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name   = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()
```
:::

::::

**Key arguments to understand:**

- `scale = 3`: It controls how much ridges overlap (higher = more overlap)
- `rel_min_height = 0.01`: It trims tails below 1% of the peak height, avoiding long flat tails
- `bandwidth = 3.4`: It controls the kernel density smoother width. The higher, the smoother. 

> **Takeaway:**
> Class 3A and 3B have distributions clustered at high scores (70-90), while 3H
> and 3I are concentrated at low scores (20-50). Notably, the *shape* differs as well, 
> 3I shows a narrow, peaked distribution (homogeneously weak), while middle
> classes like 3E-3F show broader, flatter distributions indicating mixed ability.

### Varying fill colors along the x axis

Instead of a single flat fill colour, we can let the fill **vary along the score
axis** using `geom_density_ridges_gradient()`. This adds a visual cue for score
magnitude.

:::: panel-tabset
#### The plot

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Maths score distributions with fill colour varying along the score axis.](Hands-on_Ex04_files/figure-html/fig-ridgeline-gradient-1.png){#fig-ridgeline-gradient width=672}
:::
:::


#### The code

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = MATHS,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = after_stat(x))) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(
    scale          = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name   = "Maths Score",
    option = "C"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name   = "Maths Score",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name   = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()
```
:::

::::

> **Takeaway:**
> The gradient fill immediately communicates which classes cluster at high vs low
> scores without needing to read axis values carefully. However, `fill` and
> `alpha` transparency **cannot be used together** with gradient fills, which is a
> technical limitation to take note. 

> **Code:** `after_stat(x)` is the modern replacement for the deprecated
> `stat(x)` syntax used in the course notes. Both map the computed x-axis value
> onto the fill aesthetic.


### Mapping the probabilities directly onto colour
Beside providing additional `geom` objects to support the need to plot ridgeline plot, `ggridges` package also provides a stat function called [`stat_density_ridges()`](https://wilkelab.org/ggridges/reference/stat_density_ridges.html) that replaces [`stat_density()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/geom_density.html) of ggplot2.

:::: panel-tabset
#### The plot

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Science score ridgelines coloured by tail probability (ECDF).](Hands-on_Ex04_files/figure-html/fig-ridgeline-ecdf-1.png){#fig-ridgeline-ecdf width=672}
:::
:::

#### The code

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = SCIENCE,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = 0.5 - abs(0.5 - after_stat(ecdf)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom      = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name      = "Tail\nProbability",
    direction = -1
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Science Score", expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()
```
:::

::::

> **Takeaway:**
> The expression `0.5 - abs(0.5 - ecdf)` maps values **closest to the median**
> to the highest fill value, and tail extremes to the lowest. Dark regions are
> the rare extreme scores. Bright central bands show where most students fall.
> This is especially useful for spotting which classes have fat tails (more
> outliers) vs tight central distributions.

::: callout-important
Always include `calc_ecdf = TRUE` inside `stat_density_ridges()` when using ECDF-based colour mapping, the plot will silently produce incorrect results without it.
:::

### Ridgeline plots with quantile lines
We can colour the ridgeline by **quartile bands** to make Q1, median, and Q3
immediately visible within each class's density shape. 

:::: panel-tabset 
#### Quartile Band 

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![English score ridgelines coloured by quartile band.](Hands-on_Ex04_files/figure-html/fig-ridgeline-quantile-1.png){#fig-ridgeline-quantile width=672}
:::
:::


#### The code

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = ENGLISH,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = factor(after_stat(quantile)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom           = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf      = TRUE,
    quantiles      = 4,
    quantile_lines = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Quartiles") +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "English Score", expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()
```
:::



#### Probability Band

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![English scores with 2.5% and 97.5% tail regions highlighted in red/blue.](Hands-on_Ex04_files/figure-html/fig-ridgeline-tails-1.png){#fig-ridgeline-tails width=672}
:::
:::


#### The code

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = ENGLISH,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = factor(after_stat(quantile)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom      = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name   = "Probability",
    values = c("#FF0000A0", "#A0A0A0A0", "#0000FFA0"),
    labels = c("Bottom 2.5%", "Middle 95%", "Top 2.5%")
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "English Score", expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()
```
:::


::::



## Visualising Distribution with Raincloud Plot

A **Raincloud plot** overlays three visual layers to give a complete picture of
the distribution:

| Layer | Geom | What it shows |
|---|---|---|
| Half-density ("cloud") | `stat_halfeye()` | Overall shape, skewness, modes |
| Boxplot | `geom_boxplot()` | Median, IQR, outliers |
| Dot plot ("rain") | `stat_dots()` | Individual data points, sample size |

:::: panel-tabset
#### The plot

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Raincloud plot: Maths scores by Gender.](Hands-on_Ex04_files/figure-html/fig-raincloud-gender-1.png){#fig-raincloud-gender width=672}
:::
:::


#### The code

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = GENDER,
           y = MATHS)) +
  stat_halfeye(
    adjust        = 0.5,
    justification = -0.2,
    .width        = 0,
    point_colour  = NA
  ) +
  geom_boxplot(
    width         = 0.20,
    outlier.shape = NA
  ) +
  stat_dots(
    side          = "left",
    justification = 1.2,
    binwidth      = 0.5,
    dotsize       = 1.5
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_economist()
```
:::


::::

# Funnel Plots for Fair Comparison 
## Overview

Three methods for creating **funnel plots** for fair visual comparison: 

1. The `FunnelPlotR` package (quick method)
2. `ggplot2` from scratch (manual method)
3. `plotly` + `ggplot2` for interactivity

The dataset used is **COVID-19_DKI_Jakarta.csv**, which contains cumulative COVID-19 cases and deaths by sub-district (*kelurahan*) as at 31 July 2021.

---

## Load Packages


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pacman::p_load(tidyverse, FunnelPlotR, plotly, knitr)
```
:::


**Packages used:**

| Package | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `tidyverse` | Data wrangling & `ggplot2` visualisation |
| `FunnelPlotR` | High-level funnel plot creation |
| `plotly` | Interactive visualisation |
| `knitr` | Rendering static HTML tables |

---

## Import Data


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
covid19 <- read_csv("data/COVID-19_DKI_Jakarta.csv") %>%
  mutate_if(is.character, as.factor)
```
:::

::: callout-note
The dataset covers 267 sub-districts across five cities in DKI Jakarta. Key columns are `Positive` (cumulative confirmed cases) and `Death` (cumulative deaths).
:::

Preview the first few rows:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
covid19 %>%
  head(6) %>%
  kable()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


| Sub-district ID|City            |District         |Sub-district | Positive| Recovered| Death|
|---------------:|:---------------|:----------------|:------------|--------:|---------:|-----:|
|      3172051003|JAKARTA UTARA   |PADEMANGAN       |ANCOL        |     1776|      1691|    26|
|      3173041007|JAKARTA BARAT   |TAMBORA          |ANGKE        |     1783|      1720|    29|
|      3175041005|JAKARTA TIMUR   |KRAMAT JATI      |BALE KAMBANG |     2049|      1964|    31|
|      3175031003|JAKARTA TIMUR   |JATINEGARA       |BALI MESTER  |      827|       797|    13|
|      3175101006|JAKARTA TIMUR   |CIPAYUNG         |BAMBU APUS   |     2866|      2792|    27|
|      3174031002|JAKARTA SELATAN |MAMPANG PRAPATAN |BANGKA       |     1828|      1757|    26|


:::
:::



## FunnelPlotR Methods

### Basic Funnel Plot

The simplest call to `funnel_plot()` requires only a numerator, denominator, and group variable.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
funnel_plot(
  .data       = covid19,
  numerator   = Positive,
  denominator = Death,
  group       = `Sub-district`
)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04_files/figure-html/funnelplotr-basic-1.png){width=960}
:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 0 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion. 
```


:::
:::


::: callout-tip
**What to note:**

- `group` defines the granularity of points (sub-district vs. district or city).
- Default `data_type` is `"SR"` (standardised ratio).
- The plot is automatically adjusted for overdispersion.
- Confidence limits shown are 95% and 99.8% by default.
:::

---

### Makeover 1. Switch to Proportions with Zoomed Axes

Swap numerator/denominator so we plot the **fatality rate** (deaths ÷ positives), change `data_type` to `"PR"` (proportions), and zoom the axes for readability.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
funnel_plot(
  .data       = covid19,
  numerator   = Death,
  denominator = Positive,
  group       = `Sub-district`,
  data_type   = "PR",        # proportions instead of standardised ratio
  xrange      = c(0, 6500),  # zoom x-axis
  yrange      = c(0, 0.05)   # zoom y-axis
)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04_files/figure-html/funnelplotr-makeover1-1.png){width=960}
:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 7 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion. 
```


:::
:::


::: callout-tip
`data_type = "PR"` switches the underlying statistical model to a proportion-based funnel, which is more appropriate when comparing rates (deaths per case) across units of different sizes.
:::

---

### Makeover 2. Clean Labels and Title

Remove automatic outlier labels and add descriptive axis titles and a plot title.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
funnel_plot(
  .data       = covid19,
  numerator   = Death,
  denominator = Positive,
  group       = `Sub-district`,
  data_type   = "PR",
  xrange      = c(0, 6500),
  yrange      = c(0, 0.05),
  label       = NA,           # remove outlier labels
  title       = "Cumulative COVID-19 Fatality Rate by Cumulative Total Number of COVID-19 Positive Cases",
  x_label     = "Cumulative COVID-19 Positive Cases",
  y_label     = "Cumulative Fatality Rate"
)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04_files/figure-html/funnelplotr-makeover2-1.png){width=960}
:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 7 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion. 
```


:::
:::
